properties.name: Kasumbalesa Border (DRC)
properties.wounding_strategy: The Mineral 'Neurology': Instead of attacking a nation's military bases, rivals will weaponize local labor unions, fund eco-terrorist sabotage, or buy up the specific port terminals where cobalt leaves Africa. Paralyzing these specific, narrow export routes starves the West and Asian rivals of the materials needed for EV transition and next-gen military tech.

properties.name: Kourou Spaceport (Fr. Guiana)
properties.wounding_strategy: Equatorial Aerospace Corridors: The Earth's rotation provides the most fuel-efficient trajectory for satellite launches near the equator. Heavy diplomatic and economic coercion aimed at South American nations could deny rival space agencies access to optimal launch trajectories, severely slowing orbital capabilities.

properties.name: Penang Hub (Malaysia)
properties.wounding_strategy: Secondary Tech-Packaging Hubs: Why attack Taiwan — a heavily fortified 'porcupine' — when you can fund a ransomware attack on a power grid in Malaysia? The microchips may be printed in Taipei, but if they cannot be tested and packaged in Penang, the global supply chain halts just the same, with zero geopolitical fallout.

properties.name: Strait of Hormuz (Iran Cable Threat)
properties.wounding_strategy: IRAN CABLE WARFARE DOCTRINE: Iran has explicitly threatened to sever undersea cables as retaliation for sanctions or military strikes. Iranian officials stated in 2023-2024 that 'the West's digital arteries pass through our waters.' The IRGCN fields Qaher-class underwater drones and combat diver units trained for subsea infrastructure attack. A single operation at Hormuz could simultaneously disconnect UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman — the financial hub of the Gulf — from global internet. Combined with oil supply disruption, this constitutes a dual-domain economic warfare strike with no missiles fired.
properties.strategic_context: First use validated: Iranian-backed Houthi forces cut three Red Sea cables Feb 2024 (EIG, SEACOM, AAE-1) — proving the doctrine works and Iran was willing to use proxies for cable attacks.
properties.status: ACTIVE THREAT — Iran tested proxy capability 2024

properties.name: Red Sea Cable Corridor (Houthi Zone)
properties.wounding_strategy: ACTIVE THEATER — RED SEA: The Bab-el-Mandeb strait (only 18 miles wide) is now a confirmed cable warfare zone. February 2024: Houthi forces (IRGC-directed) cut three cables simultaneously — AAE-1, EIG, and SEACOM — disrupting internet access for 1.2 billion people across Africa, India, and the Middle East. This is Iran's 'grey-zone' doctrine: use non-state proxies to attack Western digital infrastructure below the threshold of war. The Red Sea now carries 17% of global internet traffic but has zero physical defense mechanism against subsea attack.
properties.incident_history: Feb 24, 2024: Three simultaneous cuts. 17 African nations lost primary internet. East African banking degraded 8 weeks. Economic damage estimated $250M+.
properties.status: CONFIRMED — Multiple cable cuts already executed

properties.name: Chabahar-Gwadar Digital Corridor
properties.wounding_strategy: DIGITAL SILK ROAD CHOKE: Iran controls the northern shore of the Arabian Sea. China's CPEC project routes cables through Gwadar (Pakistan) adjacent to Iran. This creates a potential China-Iran digital corridor that could route traffic through infrastructure they jointly monitor. India's Chabahar port (funded partly by India to bypass Pakistan) represents a counter-move. The cable routes through this corridor could become a geopolitical leverage point — Iran threatening to 'accidentally' cut cables that do not route through its preferred infrastructure.

properties.name: Taiwan Strait Cable Nexus
properties.wounding_strategy: TAIWAN NEXUS: Taiwan sits at the crossroads of the most cable-dense shipping lane in the world. In any Taiwan Strait conflict scenario, cables would be severed within hours — either as collateral damage or deliberately. Taiwan's isolation would immediately cascade: semiconductor fab order flows halt, TSMC supply chain coordination breaks, $500B in annual tech trade freezes. In April 2023, two cables to the Matsu Islands (Taiwan outlier islands) were cut by Chinese vessels — a dress rehearsal for larger-scale digital isolation.
